content:
12 min read
2 hours ago

--

Type into a search box filtering a large list, and for a moment the keystrokes lag half a beat behind what we typed. Push that same list large enough, and the lag stops being a half beat and turns into a dialog sitting over the entire page: Page Unresponsive. Wait, or leave the page.
Neither of these is a guess on the browser’s part. Both are the browser reporting, at two different scales, the exact same fact about our own code.
What that dialog is actually reporting
A browser tab and the browser window around it, the address bar, the tabs, that unresponsive dialog itself, are not the same running program. Chrome and most browsers give each tab its own renderer process, separate from the browser’s own process, partly for security, partly so one broken tab can’t take the whole browser down with it.
The browser process keeps checking in on every renderer it’s managing, sending it a message at intervals and expecting an acknowledgment back within a certain window. The renderer can only send that acknowledgment if its main thread is free to handle it, the same main thread that’s supposed to be handling our clicks and our scrolling and our own code.
So when something on that main thread runs long enough, the renderer stops answering the…
